title: Unmatched: Little Red Riding Hood vs. Beowulf

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Fighter
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Unmatched -- where legends clash and only one walks away. This set pits Little Red Riding Hood against Beowulf in an asymmetric duel on the battlefield of Heorot. Each player picks one of these two fighters. Little Red is a melee hero with a deadly basket mechanic and her ranged Huntsman sidekick. Beowulf is a melee brawler who grows more dangerous as he takes damage through his Rage system, aided by his sidekick Wiglaf. Pick your champion and prepare for battle.

walkthrough.title: Gather Your Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes everything belonging to their hero. For Little Red: her 30-card action deck, hero miniature, character card, Little Red's Basket card, the Huntsman sidekick token, and 2 health dials. For Beowulf: his 30-card action deck, hero miniature, character card, the Wiglaf sidekick token, 2 health dials, and 3 rage tokens. If this is your first game, punch out the health dial circles and connect them with the rivets provided.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Battlefield
walkthrough.text: Choose a side of the game board and place it in the center of the table. Both sides of the board show the same battlefield -- Heorot, the great mead hall. One side uses filled-in color patterns (helpful for colorblind players), and the other uses empty colored circles. The board is divided into zones indicated by different colors. Spaces connected by lines are adjacent. Each space holds exactly one fighter. Set the 3 door tokens to the side -- all doors start open.

walkthrough.title: Set Starting Health
walkthrough.text: Check your character card for starting health values. Little Red starts at 14 HP. The Huntsman starts at 9 HP. Beowulf starts at 17 HP. Wiglaf starts at 6 HP. Set each fighter's health dial to their starting value. Fighters can never gain health above the highest number on their dial. When a health dial reads 'The End,' that fighter is at zero and is defeated.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Deck and Draw Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player shuffles their 30-card action deck thoroughly and places it face down as their draw pile. Then each player draws 5 cards as their starting hand. Keep your hand hidden from your opponent. Your hand limit is 7 cards -- you must discard down to 7 at the end of your turn, but during your turn you can temporarily hold more. Little Red also places her Basket card into her discard pile face up -- this is her starting basket item.

walkthrough.title: Place Fighters and Set Special Components
walkthrough.text: The younger player places their hero miniature on the space marked 1 on the battlefield, then places their sidekick token in a separate space within the same zone. The older player then does the same using the space marked 2. Beowulf's player takes the 3 rage tokens and places 1 token flame-side up in front of them -- Beowulf starts the game with 1 Rage. The remaining 2 rage tokens start unlit-side up. The younger player takes the first turn.

summary.title: Choose Your Fighter
bullets: Pick Little Red Riding Hood or Beowulf
Little Red: melee hero + ranged Huntsman sidekick, basket mechanic
Beowulf: melee hero + Wiglaf sidekick, Rage mechanic that grows with damage

summary.title: Gather Your Components
bullets: Little Red: 30-card deck, miniature, character card, Basket card, Huntsman token, 2 health dials
Beowulf: 30-card deck, miniature, character card, Wiglaf token, 2 health dials, 3 rage tokens
First game: assemble health dials with rivets

summary.title: Set Up the Battlefield
bullets: Place the Heorot board (either side) in the center of the table
Colored zones, adjacent spaces connected by lines, one fighter per space
Set 3 door tokens to the side -- all doors start open

summary.title: Set Starting Health
bullets: Little Red: 14 HP, Huntsman: 9 HP
Beowulf: 17 HP, Wiglaf: 6 HP
Health cannot exceed the highest value on each dial

summary.title: Prepare Your Deck and Draw Cards
bullets: Shuffle your 30-card deck, place face down as draw pile
Draw 5 cards as your starting hand (hand limit is 7 at end of turn)
Little Red places her Basket card into her discard pile face up

summary.title: Place Fighters and Set Special Components
bullets: Younger player places hero on space 1, sidekick in same zone; older player uses space 2
Beowulf starts with 1 Rage token (flame-side up), 2 unlit tokens in reserve
Younger player takes the first turn

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Two Actions per Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must take exactly 2 actions. You cannot skip an action. You may choose two different actions or the same action twice. The three possible actions are Maneuver, Scheme, and Attack. After taking both actions, check your hand size -- if you have more than 7 cards, discard down to 7. Then your opponent takes their turn. The rhythm of Unmatched is fast and brutal: move, plan, strike.

walkthrough.title: Maneuver: Draw and Move
walkthrough.text: The Maneuver action has two steps. First, you must draw the top card of your deck -- this is mandatory. Second, you may move your fighters. Your character card shows your move value. Move each of your fighters, one at a time, up to that many spaces. Fighters move through adjacent spaces only. You can move through your own fighters but not through or into enemy-occupied spaces. You do not have to move all fighters the same distance, and you can move a fighter zero spaces.

walkthrough.title: Boosting Movement
walkthrough.text: During a Maneuver, you may boost your movement by discarding 1 card from your hand. Add that card's boost value (shown on the card) to your move value for this maneuver. Ignore the discarded card's effect -- you are just using it for the boost number. This is powerful for crossing the battlefield quickly or escaping a bad position. Cards that belong to defeated fighters can still be discarded for their boost value.

walkthrough.title: Scheme: Play a Scheme Card
walkthrough.text: The Scheme action lets you play a scheme card from your hand. Declare which of your fighters is playing the scheme -- they are the active fighter, and the card must list that fighter. Resolve the card's effect immediately, then place it in your discard pile. Schemes are how you set up combos, reposition, or trigger special abilities without entering direct combat. You cannot play a scheme if all listed fighters are defeated.

walkthrough.title: Attack: Declare and Target
walkthrough.text: When you take the Attack action, declare which of your fighters is attacking and which enemy fighter they are targeting. Melee fighters (marked with crossed swords) can only attack adjacent fighters. Ranged fighters (marked with an arrow) can attack adjacent fighters or any fighter in the same zone, regardless of adjacency. You cannot attack if you have no attack cards in hand or no valid targets.

walkthrough.title: Attack: Choose Cards and Reveal
walkthrough.text: The attacker chooses an attack card from their hand and places it face down. The card must be usable by the attacking fighter. Then the defender may choose a defense card and place it face down -- or choose not to defend at all. Versatile cards (marked with a split icon) can be used as either attack or defense cards. Once both players have decided, reveal both cards simultaneously. The clash begins.

walkthrough.title: Attack: Resolve Combat
walkthrough.text: After revealing, resolve card effects in order: first 'immediately' effects, then 'during combat' effects. If both players have effects at the same timing, the defender's effect resolves first. Then calculate damage: the attacker's attack value minus the defender's defense value equals damage dealt. Reduce the defender's health by that amount. After damage, resolve any 'after combat' effects. Finally, place both played cards in their owners' discard piles.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing Combat
walkthrough.text: Some cards check who won the combat. The attacker wins the combat if they dealt at least 1 damage from the attack itself (not from card effects). The defender wins the combat if they took zero damage from the attack itself, even if they took damage from other effects. This distinction matters for 'after combat' triggers on many cards.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustion: When Your Deck Runs Out
walkthrough.text: When your deck is empty, your fighters are exhausted. If you need to draw a card while exhausted -- whether from Maneuver or a card effect -- do not reshuffle your discard pile. Instead, each of your fighters immediately takes 2 damage. This is devastating. Managing your deck is managing your life total. Draw aggressively early, but recognize when the deck is getting thin and conserve.

walkthrough.title: Defeating Fighters and Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: When any fighter is reduced to zero health, they are defeated. If a sidekick is defeated, remove their token from the battlefield immediately. If your hero is defeated, you lose the game instantly. The entire goal of Unmatched is simple: reduce your opponent's hero to zero health before they do the same to you. Sidekick elimination is a tactical choice, not the win condition -- but removing a sidekick strips your opponent of actions and card options.

walkthrough.title: Doors on the Heorot Battlefield
walkthrough.text: Heorot has 3 door tokens. Doors start open. During a Maneuver action (not other movement effects), any of your fighters can close an adjacent door for free, or spend 1 space of movement to open an adjacent door. When a door is closed, treat lines crossing it as if they do not exist -- this blocks movement, attacks, adjacency, and even splits zones apart. Doors are a powerful tactical tool for isolating enemies or protecting wounded fighters.

walkthrough.title: Card Types and Fighter Restrictions
walkthrough.text: Every card shows which fighter can use it -- hero only, sidekick only, or any fighter. Attack cards have a red banner with a value. Defense cards have a blue banner with a value. Versatile cards have a split red/blue banner and can serve as either attack or defense. Scheme cards have a yellow banner and are played as their own action. Each card also has a boost value in the bottom corner, used when discarding to boost movement or other effects. Knowing your deck composition is knowing your battlefield options.

Same action can be taken twice
Discard down to 7 cards at end of turn

Cannot move through enemy fighters; can move through your own

Cards for defeated fighters can still be discarded for boost

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Heorot
walkthrough.text: This practice tutorial walks you through the opening turns of a duel between Little Red Riding Hood and Beowulf. Player 1 controls Little Red, and Player 2 controls Beowulf. We will play through several turns, demonstrate all three action types, show off each hero's unique mechanics, and call out common mistakes that trip up new players. Set up the game following the setup steps -- Little Red on space 1 with the Huntsman nearby, Beowulf on space 2 with Wiglaf. Both players draw 5 cards. Beowulf starts with 1 Rage. Little Red's Basket card sits in her discard pile. Let the battle begin.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Little Red Maneuvers
walkthrough.text: Little Red takes her first action: Maneuver. She draws 1 card from her deck (now 6 cards in hand). Then she moves. Little Red has a move value of 2, so she can move each of her fighters up to 2 spaces. She moves Little Red 2 spaces toward the center of the board. Then she moves the Huntsman 1 space, keeping him at range in a different part of the same zone. Remember: each fighter moves one at a time, and you finish one fighter's move before starting the next.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Little Red Maneuvers Again
walkthrough.text: For her second action, Little Red takes Maneuver again. She draws another card (now 7 in hand -- right at the limit). She decides to boost her movement by discarding a card with a boost value of 2, giving her a total move value of 4 this maneuver. She pushes Little Red 3 more spaces forward and repositions the Huntsman into a zone where he can threaten Beowulf with ranged attacks. Boosting is a great way to close distance or create separation when 2 spaces is not enough.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Beowulf Maneuvers
walkthrough.text: Beowulf's turn begins. He takes the Maneuver action, drawing 1 card (now 6 in hand). Beowulf has a move value of 2. He moves Beowulf 2 spaces toward Little Red, eager to close the gap for melee combat. Then he moves Wiglaf 2 spaces along a different path, flanking from the side. Beowulf wants to get adjacent to Little Red as fast as possible -- his melee attacks hit hard, and he needs adjacency to strike.

walkthrough.title: Turn 1: Beowulf Plays a Scheme
walkthrough.text: For his second action, Beowulf plays a Scheme card. He declares Beowulf as the active fighter and plays a scheme from his hand. The scheme's effect resolves immediately -- perhaps repositioning Wiglaf or setting up a tactical advantage. Then the card goes to Beowulf's discard pile. Schemes are versatile tools: they let you trigger effects without entering combat, set up future turns, or disrupt your opponent's plans. Always check if a scheme could serve you better than a raw attack.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Little Red Attacks with the Huntsman
walkthrough.text: Little Red takes the Attack action. The Huntsman is a ranged fighter, so he can target any enemy in his zone, not just adjacent fighters. Little Red declares the Huntsman as the attacker and targets Wiglaf, who is in the same zone but not adjacent. She places an attack card face down -- one that the Huntsman is allowed to use. Beowulf's player decides to defend Wiglaf and places a defense card face down. Both cards are revealed simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Resolving the Huntsman's Attack
walkthrough.text: The attack card shows a value of 3, and the defense card shows a value of 2. The attack resolves: 3 minus 2 equals 1 damage to Wiglaf. Wiglaf's health drops from 6 to 5. Both cards go to their respective discard piles. Notice that the top card of Little Red's discard pile just changed -- that means her basket item may have changed too. Always check what is in the basket after every card hits the discard pile. The basket is a living mechanic that shifts with every play.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Little Red's Basket
walkthrough.text: The top card of Little Red's discard pile is her basket. The banner on that card shows one of three items: a knife, a pelt, or wolfsbane. Some of Little Red's cards have conditional effects that only trigger if a specific item is in her basket. For example, a card might say 'If you have a knife in your basket, deal 3 damage to an opposing fighter in your zone.' If the basket card is the special Basket card or What's That In My Basket?, it counts as a wild -- any one of the three items, but not all three at once.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 1: Forgetting to Check the Basket
walkthrough.text: On her next turn, Little Red plays an attack card with a basket effect -- she needs a knife to trigger additional damage. She excitedly announces the bonus damage, but wait -- the top card of her discard pile shows a pelt, not a knife. The basket effect does not trigger. This is the most common Little Red mistake: forgetting to verify what is actually in the basket before relying on a conditional effect. Always glance at your discard pile before committing to a play that depends on a specific basket item. The rest of the card still resolves normally, but she missed out on the bonus.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Beowulf Attacks Little Red
walkthrough.text: Beowulf is now adjacent to Little Red. He takes the Attack action, declaring Beowulf as the attacker and Little Red as the target. He places an attack card face down -- Epic Poem, with a base value of 2 and a during combat effect that adds his current Rage to the attack value. Little Red places a defense card face down. Cards are revealed. Epic Poem's during combat effect fires: Beowulf has 1 Rage, so the attack becomes 3. Little Red's defense card has a value of 2. She takes 1 damage, dropping from 14 to 13 HP.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Beowulf's Rage
walkthrough.text: Beowulf has 3 rage tokens. He started the game with 1 Rage. Whenever Beowulf himself takes damage -- from any source, at any time -- he gains 1 Rage, up to a maximum of 3. He does not gain Rage when Wiglaf takes damage, only when Beowulf personally is hit. Many of Beowulf's cards let him spend Rage for powerful bonus effects. Spending Rage is always optional. Use the flame side of the token to show active Rage, and flip to the unlit side when spent. Beowulf is a fighter who gets more dangerous as you hurt him -- a cornered wolf is the deadliest.

walkthrough.title: Turn 3: Little Red Uses a Basket Combo
walkthrough.text: Little Red maneuvers, drawing a card. She checks her discard pile -- the top card shows a knife. She moves into position adjacent to Beowulf. For her second action, she attacks Beowulf with a card that has a knife basket effect: 'If you have a knife in your basket, your opponent randomly discards a card, and add that card's boost value to your defense value.' She verified the basket first this time. The attack hits, and the basket effect triggers, forcing Beowulf to randomly lose a card from his hand. The discard reshuffles Little Red's basket to whatever card she just played.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 2: Attacking Through a Closed Door
walkthrough.text: On a later turn, Beowulf closes a door adjacent to him during a Maneuver. Little Red's player forgets the door is closed and tries to have the Huntsman attack Beowulf through the closed door, claiming they are in the same zone. But a closed door breaks zone connections -- the shortest path between them now crosses the closed door, putting them in effectively different zones. The attack is illegal. Always check door status before declaring ranged attacks. Closed doors do not just block adjacency; they fracture the entire zone map.

walkthrough.title: Using Doors Tactically
walkthrough.text: Beowulf realizes doors are a powerful tool. On his Maneuver, he closes a second door, cutting off the Huntsman from the fight entirely. Now the Huntsman cannot target any of Beowulf's fighters from his current position. Closing a door is free -- it costs zero movement. Opening a door costs 1 space of movement. Beowulf effectively neutralized a ranged threat without spending an attack. Doors can isolate enemies, protect wounded fighters, or force opponents to waste turns repositioning. Think of them as walls you can deploy at will.

walkthrough.title: The Huntsman Repositions
walkthrough.text: Little Red needs to get her Huntsman back into the action. She takes a Maneuver, draws a card, and boosts her movement by discarding a card with boost value 3. With a total move of 5, she moves the Huntsman all the way around the closed doors into a new zone where he has line of sight to Beowulf's fighters. Then she moves Little Red 2 spaces to stay threatening. Repositioning after a door play often costs an entire turn -- this is why door control is so valuable. Every turn your opponent spends maneuvering is a turn they are not attacking.

walkthrough.title: Beowulf Takes Damage and Gains Rage
walkthrough.text: Little Red attacks Beowulf with a powerful card. Beowulf plays a defense card but the attack value is higher. Beowulf takes 3 damage, dropping from 17 to 14 HP. Because Beowulf took damage, he gains 1 Rage, going from 1 to 2 Rage. Now his Rage-powered cards become significantly more dangerous. If Beowulf takes damage again this combat from an after-combat effect, he would gain yet another Rage. Remember: each instance of damage that hits Beowulf triggers a separate Rage gain, up to his maximum of 3.

walkthrough.title: Beowulf Unleashes a Rage-Fueled Strike
walkthrough.text: With 2 Rage stacked, Beowulf attacks Little Red with The Ancient Heirloom. This card lets him spend 2 Rage to boost its attack value to 5 and spend an additional Rage to also draw a card. He spends all his Rage tokens, flipping them to the unlit side. The attack value jumps to 5. Little Red plays a defense card worth 2, taking 3 damage. Her health drops from 13 to 10. Beowulf is now at 0 Rage, but the damage is done. This is the Beowulf rhythm: absorb punishment, stockpile Rage, then strike with devastating force.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 3: Forgetting Exhaustion Damage
walkthrough.text: Several turns later, both decks are getting thin. Little Red takes a Maneuver and reaches for her deck to draw -- but it is empty. Her fighters are exhausted. She does not reshuffle her discard pile. Instead, both Little Red and the Huntsman immediately take 2 damage each. That is 2 damage to her hero and 2 to her sidekick, just for trying to draw. Beowulf's player made the same mistake earlier, assuming they could reshuffle. You cannot. Once the deck is gone, every forced draw is a punishing 2 damage to all your fighters. Plan your card usage accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Effect Timing Matters
walkthrough.text: In a critical clash, both players reveal cards with 'during combat' effects. Who goes first? The defender's effect always resolves first when effects trigger at the same time. This can be game-deciding: if the defender's during-combat effect boosts their defense value, it applies before damage is calculated. If the attacker's during-combat effect boosts their attack, it also applies -- but the defender got their advantage first. Pay close attention to timing labels: 'immediately' effects happen right after reveal, 'during combat' happens next, and 'after combat' happens last.

walkthrough.title: The Feint Card
walkthrough.text: Beowulf plays Feint as a defense card. Feint has a powerful immediately effect: cancel all effects on your opponent's card. This nullifies any bonus damage, basket triggers, or other text on Little Red's attack card. The attack still resolves using its printed value minus Feint's defense value, but all the clever effects Little Red was counting on are wiped out. Feint is one of the most disruptive cards in the game. Keep it in mind both when you have it and when your opponent might have it -- do not overcommit to an effect-heavy attack if a Feint could ruin it.

walkthrough.title: Wiglaf Falls in Battle
walkthrough.text: The Huntsman lands a ranged attack on Wiglaf, bringing him to 0 HP. Wiglaf is defeated and his token is immediately removed from the battlefield. Beowulf still fights on -- only the hero's defeat ends the game. But losing Wiglaf means Beowulf has fewer fighters to maneuver, fewer cards he can play (any card listing Wiglaf as the only valid fighter is now unplayable for combat), and one less body to position on the board. Those dead cards can still be discarded for boost value though.

walkthrough.title: The Final Blow
walkthrough.text: The duel reaches its climax. Little Red is at 4 HP, Beowulf is at 5 HP. Beowulf has 3 Rage -- maximum. He attacks with everything he has, playing a high-value attack card boosted by Rage. Little Red plays her best defense card, but the math is not in her favor. The attack value exceeds her defense by 4. Little Red drops to 0 HP -- 'The End' appears on her health dial. Little Red Riding Hood is defeated. Beowulf, the legendary slayer of monsters, claims victory over the girl in the red hood. The game ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text: You have seen the full flow of an Unmatched duel: maneuvering for position, managing your hand and deck, leveraging your hero's unique mechanics, and using the Heorot doors to reshape the battlefield. The three biggest mistakes to avoid: relying on basket effects without checking your discard pile, ignoring closed doors when declaring attacks, and forgetting that exhaustion deals damage to all your fighters. Check out General Tips for more strategic guidance, the Appendix for a full glossary of terms and cards, and tap the Q&A tab anytime during your game to ask your personal GM Guide a question. Now step into Heorot and fight for glory!

Order: immediately -> during combat -> damage -> after combat
Timing determines whether defense boosts apply before damage calculation

bullets: Feint cancels all effects on your opponent's card
Attack/defense values still apply normally

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Avoid the 3 key mistakes: unchecked basket, attacks through closed doors, forgotten exhaustion
Check General Tips and Appendix for deeper strategy and card references
Ask your personal GM Guide -- tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Control the Tempo with Maneuvers
walkthrough.text: New players often rush into combat, but positioning wins games in Unmatched. A well-timed Maneuver can set up a devastating attack on your next action while keeping your opponent out of position. Drawing cards is critical too -- every Maneuver refills your hand, keeping your options alive. If you are low on cards, double-maneuver to rebuild. A fighter with no cards in hand is a fighter with no defenses. Balance aggression with card management.

walkthrough.title: Defend Wisely -- Not Always
walkthrough.text: You do not have to play a defense card when attacked. Sometimes taking a small hit is better than burning a high-value defense card. Defense cards are also some of the best cards for their boost values. If the attack coming in is only 2 or 3, consider eating the damage and saving your cards for a bigger threat. On the flip side, always defend against huge attacks -- losing 5 or 6 HP in a single hit can be the beginning of the end.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Each hero has exactly 30 cards. Learn what is in your deck. Know how many high-value attacks you have, how many defense cards, and how many schemes. Track what you have played and what might still be in the deck. If you have already played both copies of your best attack card, you know it is not coming back. If your opponent has played all their Feints, you know your effects are safe. Deck knowledge transforms Unmatched from a guessing game into a strategic battle.

walkthrough.title: Target the Hero, Not the Sidekick
walkthrough.text: Eliminating a sidekick removes a fighter from the board and limits your opponent's options, but it does not win the game. Only defeating the hero wins. Unless a sidekick is causing serious problems -- like the Huntsman harassing Beowulf from range -- focus your attacks on the enemy hero. Every point of damage on a sidekick is a point not on the hero. That said, a low-health sidekick can be worth finishing off if it only takes one action, especially if it removes your opponent's ability to use certain cards.

walkthrough.title: Use Doors to Your Advantage
walkthrough.text: The Heorot battlefield has 3 doors, and they are not just set dressing -- they reshape the entire game. Closing a door is free during a Maneuver. Opening one costs 1 movement. Use doors to cut off ranged attackers, isolate wounded fighters, or force your opponent to waste an entire turn repositioning. If you are playing Beowulf against Little Red, closing doors to neutralize the Huntsman's range is a top priority. If you are Little Red, keep doors open to give the Huntsman maximum zone coverage.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Deck Like a Life Total
walkthrough.text: Your 30-card deck is a second health bar. Every Maneuver draws from it. Some effects force additional draws. When the deck runs out, each forced draw deals 2 damage to all your fighters. That means if you have both hero and sidekick alive, every exhaustion draw costs 4 total HP. Plan ahead: if your deck is down to 5 cards and you still need to fight, stop maneuver-heavy turns and start attacking with what you have. The player who runs out of cards first is usually the player who loses.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Opponent's Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: Both discard piles are public information. Check your opponent's discard pile regularly. Count how many Feints they have used. Check if their best attack cards are gone. If you are facing Little Red, look at her basket -- it is the top card of her discard pile, and it tells you exactly which conditional effects she can trigger on her next attack. If you are facing Beowulf, count his Rage and check which Rage-spending cards he has already burned through. Information is power in Unmatched.

walkthrough.title: Boost Is a Resource, Not Waste
walkthrough.text: Every card has a boost value. When you discard a card to boost movement, you lose the card's effect but gain mobility. This trade-off is often worth it. A card that would deal 2 damage as an attack might be better used as a boost 3 to close the gap and set up a 5-damage attack next action. Think of your hand as a resource pool: some cards are best played, and some are best burned for boost. Cards belonging to defeated fighters are pure boost fuel since they cannot be played in combat anyway.

summary.title: Control the Tempo with Maneuvers
bullets: Positioning is as important as attacking
Every Maneuver draws a card -- keeps your hand healthy
Double-maneuver turns rebuild your hand when you are running low

summary.title: Defend Wisely -- Not Always
bullets: Small attacks: consider taking the damage to save defense cards
Large attacks (5+): always defend if possible
Defense cards often have high boost values -- saving them has double utility

summary.title: Know Your Deck
bullets: 30 cards per hero -- learn the composition
Track played cards via the discard pile
If all Feints are gone, your effects are safe

summary.title: Target the Hero, Not the Sidekick
bullets: Only hero defeat wins the game
Sidekick damage does not end the game -- focus on the hero
Exception: finish off a low-HP sidekick cheaply to remove card options

summary.title: Use Doors to Your Advantage
bullets: Close doors for free to cut off ranged threats and isolate fighters
Opening costs 1 movement -- your opponent wastes tempo reopening
Beowulf: close doors to limit Huntsman; Little Red: keep them open

summary.title: Manage Your Deck Like a Life Total
bullets: Empty deck = 2 damage per fighter per forced draw
With hero + sidekick alive, that is 4 total HP per draw
Stop heavy maneuvering when deck is thin -- fight with what you have

summary.title: Read Your Opponent's Discard Pile
bullets: Discard piles are public -- check them regularly
vs. Little Red: check the basket (top card) to predict her effects
vs. Beowulf: count Rage and track which Rage cards are spent

summary.title: Boost Is a Resource, Not Waste
bullets: Discarding for boost trades effect for mobility
Sometimes boost 3 to set up a 5-damage attack is better than a 2-damage play
Defeated fighter's cards are pure boost fuel -- no combat use left

walkthrough.title: Basket Sequencing for Little Red
walkthrough.text: The basket changes every time a card hits Little Red's discard pile -- from attacks, defenses, and schemes. Advanced Little Red players plan their card sequence to set up the basket item they need for their next play. If you need a knife effect next turn, play a knife-banner card this turn so it sits on top of the discard pile. Scheme cards are especially useful for basket manipulation because they resolve an effect AND set up the basket for your next action. Think two moves ahead: what do I play now, and what does that leave in the basket?

walkthrough.title: Rage Economy for Beowulf
walkthrough.text: Beowulf's Rage is his most precious resource. Do not spend it on minor effects. Save Rage for game-swinging plays: a massive attack that finishes the enemy hero, or a critical defense that keeps Beowulf alive at a key moment. At 3 Rage, Beowulf's cards become terrifyingly powerful, but spending all 3 Rage in one turn leaves you at 0 with no Rage-fueled options until you take damage again. Sometimes spending 1 Rage per turn for steady advantage is better than a single explosive 3-Rage play. Read the board state and decide: incremental pressure or knockout blow?

walkthrough.title: Huntsman Positioning and Threat Projection
walkthrough.text: The Huntsman is a ranged fighter, meaning he can hit any enemy in his zone, not just adjacent enemies. This makes his positioning crucial. Place the Huntsman in a zone that overlaps with where the enemy hero wants to be. This creates a threat radius: the opponent cannot enter that zone without exposing themselves to ranged attacks. But be careful not to overextend the Huntsman -- with only 9 HP, he is fragile. Keep him at range, behind doors when possible, and use Little Red as the frontline brawler.

walkthrough.title: The Art of Not Defending
walkthrough.text: Every defense card you play is a card you cannot use to attack or boost. Sometimes the strongest play is to take the hit and save your cards for a counterattack. This is especially true when you are playing Beowulf: taking damage fuels Rage, which fuels your next attack. If the incoming attack is low (1-3 damage), eating it as Beowulf means you gain Rage and keep your defense cards for later. Against Little Red, not defending avoids triggering her 'after combat: attacker wins' effects on certain cards. Calculate the trade-off before reflexively playing defense.

walkthrough.title: Door Control as a Win Condition
walkthrough.text: Doors are not just tactical tricks -- they can be a central strategy. As Beowulf, closing all three doors can effectively turn the battlefield into a series of isolated corridors. If you can trap the Huntsman behind a closed door, Little Red loses half her fighting force without you spending a single attack. As Little Red, keeping doors open is vital for the Huntsman's zone coverage. If a door closes, prioritize reopening it or moving the Huntsman before Beowulf seals another path. The player who controls the doors controls the pace of the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Deck Burn and Exhaustion Timing
walkthrough.text: In long games, deck exhaustion can decide the winner. Track both decks mentally. If your opponent is drawing more cards than you (double maneuvering, card effects), they will hit exhaustion first. You can actively push for this by playing defensively and forcing your opponent to maneuver toward you. Each exhaustion draw deals 2 damage per fighter -- if the opponent still has both hero and sidekick, that is 4 damage per draw. In a close game, being 3-4 cards ahead in deck count can be the equivalent of 6-8 damage advantage.

walkthrough.title: Feint Timing and Mind Games
walkthrough.text: Feint is one of the most powerful cards in the game: it cancels all effects on your opponent's card. Both heroes have access to Feint (Beowulf has 3 copies). The question is when to use it. Save Feint for moments when your opponent plays a card that is effect-heavy rather than value-heavy. Feint against a card with bonus damage effects or card-draw effects can swing the game. But if your opponent plays a straight high-value attack with no effects, Feint's cancel ability is wasted. Also consider the mind game: if your opponent knows you might have Feint, they may avoid playing their best effects, which is itself a form of advantage.

walkthrough.title: Sidekick Sacrifice Timing
walkthrough.text: Your sidekick is expendable -- your hero is not. Sometimes the best play is to deliberately position your sidekick to absorb an attack that would otherwise hit your hero. Wiglaf with 6 HP can soak a couple of hits that would be devastating to Beowulf. The Huntsman at 9 HP can serve as a ranged threat AND a damage sponge. When your sidekick is low on health, consider whether keeping them alive for one more turn is worth the card resources you are spending to defend them, or whether those cards would be better spent on your hero's offense.

walkthrough.title: Hand Size as Information
walkthrough.text: Both players' hand sizes are public. If your opponent has 7 cards, they have maximum options for attack and defense. If they are down to 2 cards, they may not be able to both attack and defend. Exploit low hand sizes: attack aggressively when your opponent is card-starved -- they may have to choose between defending and saving a card for their own attack. Conversely, build up your hand before launching your offensive. The player with more cards has more options, more flexibility, and more combat rounds before exhaustion.

walkthrough.title: Asymmetric Matchup Awareness
walkthrough.text: Little Red and Beowulf play fundamentally differently, and understanding your opponent's strategy is as important as executing your own. Little Red wants to keep distance, use the Huntsman's range, manipulate the basket for bonus effects, and whittle Beowulf down while avoiding big Rage-fueled hits. Beowulf wants to close distance, shut down the Huntsman with doors, absorb damage to build Rage, and land devastating haymakers. If you are losing, ask yourself: am I playing my opponent's game instead of my own? Reposition, recalibrate, and force the fight onto your terms.

summary.title: Basket Sequencing for Little Red
bullets: Plan card play order to set up the basket item you need next turn
Scheme cards are ideal for basket manipulation
Think two moves ahead: what you play now determines your next basket

summary.title: Rage Economy for Beowulf
bullets: Save Rage for game-swinging plays, not minor effects
3 Rage = devastating, but spending it all leaves you vulnerable
Incremental 1-Rage spending can be better than a single explosive turn

summary.title: Huntsman Positioning and Threat Projection
bullets: Place Huntsman in zones overlapping enemy hero's likely path
Creates a threat radius that limits opponent's movement
Keep Huntsman at range behind doors -- 9 HP makes him fragile

summary.title: The Art of Not Defending
bullets: Every defense played is a card you cannot attack or boost with
Beowulf gains Rage from damage -- sometimes eating hits is profitable
Calculate the trade-off: saved defense card vs. lost HP

summary.title: Door Control as a Win Condition
bullets: Beowulf: close doors to isolate the Huntsman from the fight
Little Red: keep doors open for Huntsman zone coverage
Controlling doors controls the pace of the entire game

summary.title: Deck Burn and Exhaustion Timing
bullets: Track both decks -- the player who exhausts first is at a massive disadvantage
Play defensively to force opponent into more maneuvers (more draws)
3-4 card lead in deck count = 6-8 damage advantage from exhaustion

summary.title: Feint Timing and Mind Games
bullets: Save Feint for effect-heavy cards, not raw-value attacks
Beowulf has 3 Feints -- the threat of Feint is itself an advantage
Track whether your opponent's Feints have been played via their discard pile

summary.title: Sidekick Sacrifice Timing
bullets: Sidekicks are expendable -- heroes are not
Position sidekick to absorb attacks meant for the hero
When sidekick is low HP, weigh defense card cost vs. letting them fall

summary.title: Hand Size as Information
bullets: Hand size is public -- exploit opponents with few cards
Attack when opponent is card-starved; build up before your own offensive
More cards = more options, more flexibility, longer before exhaustion

summary.title: Asymmetric Matchup Awareness
bullets: Little Red: range, basket combos, and attrition
Beowulf: close distance, doors, Rage-fueled bursts
If losing, ask: am I playing my opponent's game? Reposition and recalibrate

entries.definition: One of three possible actions. Draw 1 card (mandatory), then move your fighters up to your move value (optional). You may also boost movement during this action. Doors can only be opened or closed during a Maneuver.

One of three possible actions. Play a scheme card from your hand, declaring which of your fighters is the active fighter. Resolve the effect, then discard the card. Cannot be played if the listed fighter is defeated.

One of three possible actions. Declare an attacking fighter and a target. Attacker plays an attack card face down; defender may play a defense card face down. Reveal simultaneously, resolve effects, calculate damage, then discard both cards.

During a Maneuver, discard 1 card from your hand and add its boost value (bottom corner of the card) to your move value. Some card effects also allow boosting attack or defense values. Cards for defeated fighters can still be discarded for boost.
A card with a red banner showing an attack value. Can only be played by the attacker during combat. Each card specifies which fighter may use it.
A card with a blue banner showing a defense value. Can only be played by the defender during combat. Subtracts its value from the attacker's damage.
A card with a split red/blue banner that can be used as either an attack card or a defense card. Also counts as both types for the purposes of other game effects.
A card with a yellow banner played as a Scheme action. Resolves its effect immediately and is then discarded. Not used during combat.
A card effect that resolves right after cards are revealed in combat, before damage is calculated. Triggers before 'during combat' and 'after combat' effects.
A card effect that resolves after 'immediately' effects but before damage is calculated. If both players have during combat effects, the defender's resolves first.
A card effect that resolves after damage has been calculated and applied. Even if a fighter is defeated by the combat damage, after combat effects still resolve (unless the game ends).
The attacker won the combat if they dealt at least 1 damage from the attack value itself (not from card effects). Relevant for 'after combat' triggers that check the combat result.
The defender won the combat (attacker lost) if the defender took zero damage from the attack value, even if they took damage from card effects.
Two spaces connected by a line on the battlefield are adjacent. Adjacency determines melee attack range and various card effects. Closed doors remove adjacency between the spaces on either side.
A group of spaces on the battlefield indicated by the same color. Ranged fighters can target enemies anywhere in their zone. Closed doors can split a zone into separate zones.
Heorot has 3 doors. Close an adjacent door for free during a Maneuver; open one by spending 1 movement. Closed doors block movement, adjacency, attacks, and split zones along the shortest path.
A circular position on the battlefield. Each space can hold exactly one fighter. Fighters move between adjacent spaces.
Your primary fighter, represented by a miniature. When your hero is reduced to 0 HP, you lose the game. Heroes have a special ability listed on their character card.

Your secondary fighter, represented by a token. When a sidekick is defeated, remove the token from the battlefield. Losing a sidekick does not end the game but limits your card options and board presence.
A fighter marked with the crossed-swords icon. Can only attack adjacent fighters. Both Little Red and Beowulf are melee fighters.
A fighter marked with the arrow icon. Can attack adjacent fighters or any fighter in the same zone, regardless of adjacency. The Huntsman is a ranged fighter.
Tracks each fighter's current HP. Cannot exceed the highest number on the dial. When the dial reads 'The End,' the fighter is at 0 HP and is defeated.
Shows a hero's stats: starting health for hero and sidekick, move value, attack type (melee/ranged), and special ability. Keep it visible during play for reference.
Your face-up pile of played and discarded cards. Both players may inspect either discard pile at any time. For Little Red, the top card is her basket.
When your deck is empty, your fighters are exhausted. Any forced card draw instead deals 2 damage to each of your fighters. The discard pile is never reshuffled.
7 cards. You may exceed 7 during your turn, but must discard down to 7 at the end of your turn. Discarded cards go to your discard pile.

A fighter reduced to 0 HP. Sidekick tokens are removed from the board. If a hero is defeated, that player loses immediately. Cards for defeated fighters cannot be played in combat but can still be discarded for boost.

Melee hero with 14 starting HP and move value 2. Special ability: the top card of her discard pile is her basket, containing whichever item (knife, pelt, or wolfsbane) is on that card's banner. Basket items trigger conditional effects on many of her cards.
Little Red's ranged sidekick with 9 starting HP. Can attack enemies anywhere in his zone, not just adjacent. Provides range pressure and zone control for Little Red's team.

The top card of Little Red's discard pile. Contains one of three items based on the card's banner: knife, pelt, or wolfsbane. Basket effects on cards only trigger if the correct item is showing. The special Basket card and What's That In My Basket? are wild (any one item).
One of three basket items for Little Red. Knife effects typically force the opponent to randomly discard a card and add that card's boost value to Little Red's defense.
One of three basket items for Little Red. Pelt effects typically provide defensive bonuses or damage mitigation.
One of three basket items for Little Red. Wolfsbane effects typically deal additional damage or provide offensive boosts.
The Basket card and What's That In My Basket? card show all three items. When one of these is on top of the discard pile, it counts as any ONE of the three items (not all three at once).

One of Little Red's key cards. Has a conditional knife basket effect. If the basket contains a knife, deals damage to an opposing fighter in the same zone. Demonstrates how basket items interact with zone-based effects.

A defense card for Little Red. During combat, if there is a knife in the basket, opponent randomly discards a card and its boost value is added to the defense value. Can dramatically swing combat math.
A scheme card for Little Red (x2 copies). Places itself into the discard pile as a wild basket item, giving Little Red access to any basket effect on her next play. Also used for basket manipulation.

A powerful attack card for Little Red. Deals base attack damage, and after combat, deals additional damage to the defending fighter. Forces Beowulf to gain extra Rage from the after-combat damage as well.

Melee hero with 17 starting HP and move value 2. Special ability: Rage. Starts with 1 Rage (max 3). Gains 1 Rage each time Beowulf himself takes damage. Many of his cards spend Rage for powerful bonus effects.
Beowulf's melee sidekick with 6 starting HP. Fights alongside Beowulf in close combat. Losing Wiglaf does not trigger Rage and removes a body from the battlefield.

Beowulf's unique mechanic. Tracked with 3 flame tokens. Starts at 1, max 3. Beowulf gains 1 Rage each time he personally takes damage (not Wiglaf). Spent for powerful card effects. Spending is always optional.
Physical token used to track Beowulf's Rage. Flame side up = active Rage. Unlit side = spent or not yet gained. Beowulf has 3 total tokens.

An attack card for Beowulf. During combat, adds Beowulf's current Rage to the attack value. Does not spend Rage -- the Rage stays. One of Beowulf's most efficient cards since it scales without resource cost.

A defense card for Beowulf. During combat, you may spend 1 Rage to set the card's defense value to 5 and spend an additional Rage to draw a card. Extremely powerful defensive option when Rage is available.
A defense card in Beowulf's deck. Represents Beowulf's legendary resilience. Provides solid defense value for Beowulf in melee combat.

A versatile card available to Beowulf (x3 copies). Immediately: cancel all effects on your opponent's card. The attack/defense values still apply normally, but all text effects are nullified. One of the game's most disruptive cards.
A card that provides a movement effect outside of the Maneuver action. Note: doors cannot be opened or closed during Skirmish movement, only during Maneuver actions.
The number of spaces each of your fighters can move during a Maneuver action. Both Little Red and Beowulf have a move value of 2. Can be increased by boosting.
